creator: se@FreeBSD.org
text:
(In reply to Tatsuki Makino from comment #17)

> For -t, the manpage says
> RECOMMENDED FOR USE ONLY WHEN NEEDED, NOT ROUTINELY.
> So it does not seem to be a good idea.

I'm repeating myself:

I do not recommend to add RUN_DEPENDS to the dependencies that are considered when building a port. I have multiple times suggested to fix the ports by adding dependencies required at build time to BUILD_DEPENDS.

You seem to insist on a solution that treats RUN_DEPENDS same as BUILD_DEPENDS while a port is being built. This is provided by "-t" or PM_THOROUGH=yes.

I do not recommend this as a default, the port should be fixed.

> If this and similar problems are reported again, the following response would be sufficient
> 
> * use -t.

This is a possibility for ports that lack the required BUILD_DEPENDS definition.
It causes a lot of extra work for portmaster to scan for the extra dependencies.
I'd only use it to build individual ports, not with "-a".

> * wait for the next generation of portmaster to be created :)

Sorry, no, this is a fundamental problem in the way dependencies are calculated and treated in a tool like portmaster.

I could trivially add RUN_DEPENDS to BUILD_DEPENDS when scanning for dependencies. It will significantly slow down portmaster and it may cause dependency loops (depending on which ports in need of updating you have installed). It would be equivalent to adding the -t option, and this option is already available.

See: you can either treat RUN_DEPENDS as BUILD_DEPENDS, causing the negative effects mentioned above, or you can require BUILD_DEPENDS to cover everything required to build a port. There is no third possibility, therefore do not wait for the next generation of portmaster - it will behave exactly like the current one (otherwise it would be like the current version invoked with "-t").

--> The actual solution for this issue is to report the failed port to the respective maintainer and ask him to add a BUILD_DEPENDS line.

> It may be easier to have poudriere create all the necessary packages and install them with pkg add :)

Yes, poudriere works differently, and if you have the resources and want reproducible package builds, poudriere may be your best choice.

But in fact, poudriere may run into dependency loops when building ports with non-default options, since it wants all run dependencies to be in the build jail when a port build starts. And we have a number of ports that cause such dependency loops when built with non-default options.
